title: ComfyUI Tutorial Series Ep 48: LTX 0.9.7 – Turn Images into Video at Lightning Speed! ⚡
description:
Welcome to Episode 48 of the ComfyUI Tutorial Series! In this video, you'll learn how to install, configure, and use the latest LTX 0.9.7 model to create high-quality videos from still images—entirely for free and locally on your PC.

This is a major update since Episode 37: faster generation, improved quality, support for distilled models, advanced workflows, and custom nodes. Perfect for beginners and advanced users alike looking to push the limits of video generation with ComfyUI.

📌 What You’ll Learn in This Tutorial:
- How to install the LTX 0.9.7 distilled model
- Workflow setup for image-to-video conversion
- Upscaling techniques for higher quality results
- Adding multiple input frames for motion control
- Prompt generation tips with ChatGPT
- Adapting workflows for landscape, square, and portrait video ratios
- VRAM optimization tips for low-end GPUs
- How to extend video length with the new node group

Get the workflows and instructions from discord for free
https://discord.g
transcript: Welcome to episode 48 of our Comfy UI tutorial series.  Today, I will show you how to install and use the latest update of the LTX video model.  We talked about it before in episode 37, but now we have a faster model with better quality,  different nodes, upscaling, and other cool features that let us create videos locally for free in  Comfy UI. On their hugging face page, you can find more information about the model. They added a  few extra versions like a distilled version that is faster, and today we'll use one of those.  We'll also install custom nodes to work better with the models. They've updated the nodes to  support the newest versions of the models, including the distilled ones, a few loris, some upscalers,  and more. So, let's open Comfy UI. As always, go to manager, scroll down, and check that you have  a recent version, usually it's at most a few days old. If it's not updated, click the update  all button. If that sometimes doesn't work, go to the update folder and run the update Comfy UI  bat file. Let's get started by uploading a workflow. I modified some of the recommended workflows  they suggested, and I'll add them on Discord so you can download them for free. Let's start with a  landscape one. I created workflows for landscape, square, and portrait ratios, and you can test to  see which works best for you, and check the settings I used. All those new nodes and settings might  be quite complex and are still new to me. There are some settings I don't fully understand,  so I left them on default or recommended values, but if you find better settings, feel free to share  them in the comments or on Discord. As you're used to by now, I put everything you need to get  started in this Pixaroma node. For the model, I use the FP8 distilled version. You can download it  from here and place it in the checkpoints folder. Let me quickly show you. Go to your Comfy UI folder,  then into the models folder, find the checkpoints folder, and put the model there. That model will  load here. For the clip model, depending on your memory, you can choose a bigger or smaller one.  Place that model in the text encoder folder and load it in this node. Then we use an upscaler,  which you place in the upscale models folder. If you want to find more versions of the main model,  you can click here and check the different versions they offer. I use this one because the others  were too big for me. You can also try the non distilled version like this one,  but you'll need a different workflow for that. They provide a few workflow recommendations on their  page. Once you have all the models installed, you can move on to the next step, custom nodes.  You should already have the first three nodes if you followed my episodes, but now we need two  extra nodes. If your screen is big enough, you can move the canvas to the left to see everything.  I'm using a smaller window for recording, so I can't really see all the text. Go to manager,  then custom nodes manager, search for video helper, and install it, then search for easy use,  and install that. Next, search for RG3, and install it, and finally search for LTX, and install  that one too. You should see an install button for all of them, like in these examples. Mine are  already installed. The last one is KJ nodes. Once you have all those installed, you should see a restart  button. Click it and wait for the app to restart. Now you should have everything you need.  If you want more info about LTX, you can check GitHub or Hugging Face.  So, are you ready? I split the workflow into two parts. In the first part, we generate the video  from an image. You can see the label. It's an LTX workflow FP8 version image to video. You  input an image at this size and get a video of the same size. Then, with an upscaler, you get  double the size. The second part is the upscaler. You don't need to understand everything to use it.  Let's upload an image. This is for three to two ratio images. If you use a different image ratio,  it will be cropped. I'll show you later how to change that to get a 16-9 ratio. Then you add a prompt  where you describe the subject, what it does, how it moves, the camera, and so on. I added green  color to the nodes you can edit and the rest you can leave on default settings. You can see all the  nodes I added plus two nodes here as well. Of course, you can play with the others.  But just note down the default settings so you can return to them if something goes wrong.  Let's look at this base sampler. You can set the width and height for it. It's recommended to  use multiples of 32 or 64 to get the best results. And the best results come at sizes under  1280 by 720 pixels. Any image you upload will be resized to this size. And if the ratio is different,  you can enable crop so it doesn't get distorted. Adjusting the CRF value affects the perceived  motion and fluidity of the generated videos. A higher CRF can add subtle noise which helps reduce  issues like static or overly smooth frames, making the motion feel more natural. For example,  setting a CRF between 20 and 30 is recommended to add slight variations that improve motion  perception in the output video. Then we have the number of frames which decides how long the generated  video will be depending on the frame rate. Here's how you calculate it. I like to use four seconds,  so multiply the frames per second by the number of seconds, then add an extra frame. For example,  24 frames per second times, four seconds is 96 plus one makes 97 frames total. You add that number  here in this node. The frame rate is set here in the conditioning node and also here in the video  combine node. You can use more seconds, but longer videos start to get more artifacts and also  take way longer to generate since there are more frames. We have these extra nodes with settings  that worked okay on default, but honestly, it's too much info for my brain to figure out. They  need to be in a certain format, so maybe just experiment with the numbers. They might have more  details on their GitHub page, but this is already a lot to take in. In short, open the workflow you  want, upload the image, add a prompt, click run, and let's see what happens. The first time will be  slower because it has to load the model. This one is almost 16 gigabytes. Then it loads the clip  model that helps with prompt understanding, which clip model loads depends on which one you  downloaded the bigger or smaller one. After that, everything goes to this base sampler. Similar to  the K sampler, it will generate all the individual frames 97 in total. Then the decoding part takes  the longest. You can see my GPU usage rising since I'm recording too. I have an RTX 4090 video card,  and if you look here, the V-RAM is almost full. I added this clean V-RAM node that clears memory  before the next step, but you can delete it if you want to speed things up. It takes about three  seconds to clear. This is the final result. Our cute little bunny animation from a single image  at the size we set here. If you try running the workflow again, you'll see it won't work the  second time because the seed is fixed so we can upscale later. Change the seed by using the left  and right arrows or just click and type a random number. Then click run again and it will generate a  new version. And here is the new version. If you toggle the bottom panel with this button, you can  see it took 23 seconds to generate this video from an image the second time. So it's really fast.  For some models, it takes longer to generate an image. Let's try a different seed since the  existing video has a mistake on the bunny's mouth. The result is this one. As you can see, prompt  understanding isn't always perfect, but there is some motion. Let's say we're happy with this  and want to upscale. I added this fast group mutter node here, and you just click it to enable  the upscale. Now the entire group is active. All you have to do is click run. It will only do the  upscale. It's not regenerating the first part because the seed is fixed. It takes the preview  result and runs it through the tiled sampler. Also using this spatial upscaler model you downloaded  from here, depending on your VRAM, you might want to adjust and use lower values like 768 or 512  pixels for tile size and 32 for the other values. Here, I added a simple upscale image size node  in case the size is different to get a more consistent size. This will make more sense later  when we talk about the 16-9 ratio. For now, I said it to double the original video size. There's  some grain and saturation added, but you can bypass that node if you don't need it. This is the  final upscaled result. We have a new video with this size. You can run the upscaler multiple times.  The videos are saved to the output folder. You'll have the original simple video and another  with up in the title from the upscaler. If you check properties and details, you can see the size  of the file. For your next project, remember to turn off the upscaler and generate a new version  with a new prompt, new image, and so on. What if we want multiple frames? Let me show you how to  adapt for that. I also created a separate workflow for you. Say for the first frame, I want this  red car. I can copy this load image node or just add a new one. Then I add another image for the  end frame. That's the first frame and the end frame. How do we bring both images into the workflow?  We use a batch images node. You can have multiple batch images if you have multiple images. In the  original workflow, the image was connected to the sampler both in the first part and in the second  part with the upscaler. You can move the node around to see better where it's connected and the  link turns white. Now that they're connected, we set the first image  to the first input and the second image to the second input. We've set the images, but we need to  do extra settings. First, let's add a prompt. You can use chat GPT to help generate prompts. If you  upload the two images and explain what you want, but let's keep it simple for now. In both samplers,  we need to change the indices. Right now, it's set to zero, meaning the image is used for the first  frame of the video. Since coding starts counting from zero, not one. We need to do the same here.  To avoid typing the values twice every time, let's use another node that gives the same value to  both samplers. I connect this positive node to the first sampler. Don't worry if it looks a bit  fast in the video. You can see in the final workflow, you download exactly how everything is connected.  The same positive node is connected to the second sampler as well. Now, whatever value  I input goes to both nodes in those fields. So in this case, we only have two frames. The first  and the last. The first frame will always be zero. And for the second frame, you can put it anywhere  you want in the middle or at the end by specifying the frame number. We have 97 frames in this workflow  because we wanted four seconds of video. Remember, counting starts at zero, so the last frame is  actually 96. After a comma, we add 96. If I put 50 instead, it would be somewhere in the middle of  the video. You can have multiple frames like this. Let me turn off the upscaler and run it to see  if we get any errors. Here's the result. The camera is a little shaky, but not bad for the first try.  Let me change the seed and try again. There's some camera movement here, so it doesn't always work  exactly how you expect, but at this speed, you can try a lot of versions. Let's try another seed.  This one doesn't look bad. I'll go with this one, enable the upscaler and run the workflow.  The upscaler uses a lot of VRAM, so remember to adjust the tile size if it uses too much.  You can also change the horizontal and vertical tiles to two instead of one here.  This is the result, not bad for the time it took to generate. Hopefully, they improve prompt  understanding even more. Let's open another workflow. This one is for 16-9 Landscape Ratio.  The first video is generated at this size, and the final video is upscaled to full HD.  If we look at the base sampler, you can see the size I put here.  This resolution is close to a 16-9 ratio, but if you want a more exact one, use 10-24 by 576 pixels.  I tried that, but the workflow was much slower for me, so I decided to go with a smaller size that's  faster. For this one, I have a panda taking a bath and a long prompt. Let me show you how you can  get long prompts using chatGPT. I included an example formula here that you can improve.  I mostly use a custom chatGPT trained for this, but this formula will do for now.  I pasted the formula, and it seemed to understand what I needed, giving me a random prompt.  You can ask for prompts, however you want, or upload an image. I uploaded the panda image and  pressed enter without any prompt, but you can add your own to guide it. It gave me this long prompt.  If it's too long, you can ask for half the length or less detail. Let's say I want the camera  on a tripod and the panda playing with balls. I got a new prompt and put it to the test.  I pasted it here for a four-second video with those sizes and clicked run.  By the way, this was the original prompt in the workflow and it gave me this result. For the new  prompt, I got this one. Now it has a bit more movement, but still not very complex. Let's make that movement more obvious  by changing the prompt. I got a new prompt and the result is this. I expected to pick an existing  ball from there not to make it appear a new one, but it probably didn't recognize it. By default, you'll get a similar size,  but I want it to be exact. I also enabled crop to make sure the video doesn't get distorted.  It will only crop a few pixels, so it shouldn't affect the result much. Then I ran it to get a bigger  version. Let me show you how I did the portrait workflow. First, I upload a portrait image,  and as you can see, the width and height are reversed. I changed the size here to match that,  but remember, it needs to be a multiple of 32. I made up a quick short prompt  and ran it. This time, it's not what I expected. You can try a different seed or a different prompt  to see if you get better results. I tried again with chat GPT for another prompt to see if it can do  better, and the result was much improved. Since this workflow was originally arranged for  landscape as the upscale button was covered, but the workflow I'll give you is a range for portrait,  so you won't have that problem. With the upscaler enabled, you can get a bigger version that often  fixes small issues. I switched the size here since it's portrait, and now we can run the workflow.  Here's also a square ratio workflow if you want to make videos for Instagram, but portrait  ratio is probably better for visibility there. I use 704 pixels for this one, but you can try  different sizes as long as there are multiples of 32. The result looked something like this.  LTX has improved a lot since the last model, maybe in a few months it could compete with paid models.  Here's a version that lets you extend a video. The first part of the workflow is the same as before.  What's different is the second group, which lets you extend the video. I didn't include an upscaler  for this one because it was really slow or got stuck, so maybe you'll have better luck. What it does  is take the previous generation and use this extended sampler to create new frames.  For frame overlap, you can use the number of frames per second. The number of new frames is set  to 120, which adds about 5 more seconds to the original 4 second video for a total of 9 seconds.  You can set it to 121 to get a 10 second video if you're willing to wait.  The result looks something like this. A cute extended version of the bunny video. For those with  low V RAM, try this Guff version workflow. What's different here? You need a new model and a new  node that can load that type of model. There are more versions available. If you have more memory, you can try a  bigger one, the bigger the Q number, the bigger the size, and better the quality. For the clip model,  I tried the small one. You also need a VAE model that you download separately since it's not  included here. No checkpoint. This model loads in this node. Don't forget the upscaler model.  That's it. Restart and you should be good to go. I uploaded a portrait of a woman here with  these settings. If you still can't run it, try smaller sizes. Use 512  for tile size and maybe set to horizontal and vertical tiles instead of one to see if that helps. There's a small glitch on the eye and  the original image wasn't the best either. Let's try running the upscaler again with a different  C to see if it fixes that. Okay, this one seems to have better eyes, so I'll go with this version.  Let me show you how I do a project from start to finish. First, I open a text to image workflow  like the flux mania workflow we used in episode 46. I want to make a short, so I'm going with a  9-16 ratio. For the prompt, I asked chat GPT for a prompt about an alien woman. You can always  give chat GPT more suggestions to improve the prompt. Once I had a prompt, it was time to test. I asked chat GPT to give me a shorter prompt, thinking maybe the original  was too long and got lost. The result improved. I was almost there, but something was missing.  I wanted to make things more obvious and add a smile. I got a new prompt, and finally,  the result I was after. Much better, less boring, with more emotion. What do you think?  That's all for today. If you found something useful, please leave a like and a comment to help  my channel. Let me know which model worked on your video card and what settings you prefer.
